coredata.dc:title: Solar-mediated oil-spill cleanup by a carbon dot-polyurethane sponge
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Seema
Jelinek, Raz
coredata.dc:description: Development of efficient, inexpensive, and environmentally-benign technologies for oil-spill cleanup is essential in light of the prevalence of spilling incidents, and their adverse environmental impact. We report the synthesis of a new composite material for solar-mediated oil absorbance and removal comprising highly porous polyurethane sponge embedded with a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) layer and carbon dots. The excellent oil cleanup capabilities of the material emanate from integrating the distinctive features of the three components. The PU sponge endows resilient, highly porous morphology, while the PDMS constitutes a highly hydrophobic internal layer facilitating oil absorption. The carbon dots have a particularly important role, exhibiting efficient sunlight absorbance and converting the irradiation to heat. The dissipated heat induced lower oil viscosity and concomitant greater uptake by the porous sponge. Altogether, excellent solar-mediated oil absorption and recovery, and good recycling capacity were accomplished. The simple synthesis utilizing inexpensive and environmentally-benign constituents, chemical stability, and recyclability make the carbon dot-PDMS-polyurethane composite sponge an attractive and practical vehicle for oil-spill cleanup, oil storage, and recovery applications.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation of the carbon-dot-PDMS-polyurethane composite and usage for oil-spill cleanup. The PU sponge (pinkish appearance, top left) is treated with C-dots and PDMS, resulting in a black C-dot-PDMS-PU composite matrix (middle image). Sunlight irradiation induces C-dot-emitted heating, affecting lower oil viscosity and its uptake into the sponge. Oil recovery and recycling of the sponge can be subsequently carried out (right). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Characterization of the carbon-dot-PDMS-polyurethane composite. (A) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the C-dots alone (i); PDMS-PU (ii), and the C-dot-PDMS-PU sponge (iii). (B) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the untreated PU sponge (left images), and C-dot-PDMS-PU (right); the bottom images depict magnified regions of the sponge surfaces.

Cooperative properties of the carbon dot-PDMS-polyurethane sponges. (A) Water contact angle (WCA) of C-dot-PDMS-PU sponges synthesized with different concentrations of C-dots: 1.5 mg/mL C-dot concentration in the reaction mixture (C-dot 1.5-PDMS-PU); 3 mg/mL (C-dot 3-PDMS-PU); 6 mg/mL (C-dot 6-PDMS-PU). Digital images of WCA are shown at the top of the panel. (B) Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the composite PU sponges. (C) Density, and (D) Porosity of the composite sponges. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Solar-induced heating of carbon dot-PDMS-polyurethane sponges. Temperatures were recorded using an IR camera upon solar irradiation (intensity of 1 kW/m2) at the times indicated. Representative IR images are shown above the graphs. In all graphs: pristine PU sponge (black curve)), PDMS-PU (cyan), C-dot 1.5-PDMS-PU (green), C-dot 3-PDMS-PU (blue), C-dot 6-PDMS-PU (red). (A) Sponge in air (without oil adsorption); (B) Sponges immersed in castor oil; (C) Sponges immersed in silicone oil. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Oil absorption and recovery using carbon-dot-PDMS-polyurethane composites. (A) Photographs of castor oil stained with Sudan III placed on water (left, initial stage), and following placement of a C-dot-3-PDMS-PU sponge and 5-min light irradiation (1 kW/m2 intensity). (B) Oil absorption capacity of the sponges (weight of oil absorbed per weight of bare sponge) without irradiation (green bars) and upon 30-min solar irradiation at 1 kW/m2 intensity (red bars). i. immersion in castor oil; ii. immersion in silicone oil. (C) Castor oil absorption and recovery upon several cycles using a C-dot-3-PDMS-PU sponge. (D) Photographs showing absorption selectivity of C-dot 3-PDMS-PU sponge placed within a flask containing hexane (stained with Sudan III) and water (stained with methyl violet). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

head.ce:title: Solar-mediated oil-spill cleanup by a carbon dot-polyurethane sponge

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ilse Katz Institute for Nanotechnology, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, 1 Ben Gurion Ave, Beer Sheva, 8410501, Israel
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ilse Katz Institute for Nanotechnology, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, 1 Ben Gurion Ave, Beer Sheva, 8410501, Israel

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Israel.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of efficient, inexpensive, and environmentally-benign technologies for oil-spill cleanup is essential in light of the prevalence of spilling incidents, and their adverse environmental impact. We report the synthesis of a new composite material for solar-mediated oil absorbance and removal comprising highly porous polyurethane sponge embedded with a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) layer and carbon dots. The excellent oil cleanup capabilities of the material emanate from integrating the distinctive features of the three components. The PU sponge endows resilient, highly porous morphology, while the PDMS constitutes a highly hydrophobic internal layer facilitating oil absorption. The carbon dots have a particularly important role, exhibiting efficient sunlight absorbance and converting the irradiation to heat. The dissipated heat induced lower oil viscosity and concomitant greater uptake by the porous sponge. Altogether, excellent solar-mediated oil absorption and recovery, and good recycling capacity were accomplished. The simple synthesis utilizing inexpensive and environmentally-benign constituents, chemical stability, and recyclability make the carbon dot-PDMS-polyurethane composite sponge an attractive and practical vehicle for oil-spill cleanup, oil storage, and recovery applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Oil-spill accidents during extraction, storage, and transportation are considered major environmental hazards, affecting water pollution and having long-lasting adverse ecological and health impacts [1,2]. Fast and effective removal of the bulk of oil is essential to prevent further spread and environmental damage of oil spills [3,4]. Varied mechanical, chemical, and biological methods have been introduced to combat oil spills. Those strategies include the use of skimmers [5], oil dispersion agents designed to breakdown the oil into small droplets [6], biodegradation [7], and in situ burning [8]. Absorbent technologies have been employed in oil spill cleanup applications due to the low cost, relatively easy synthesis schemes, and recyclability [9,10]. In this regard, many natural and synthetic absorbents such as chitosan, cellulose, superhydrophobic aerogels, and cross-linked polymers exhibiting high porosity and oleophilic properties have been used for oil absorption and removal [11–13]. A recurring major challenge in many of the oil absorption materials reported or commercially-implemented, however, is their inadequate performance for oils exhibiting high viscosity. Such oils often do not diffuse well into the absorbent matrixes and thus cannot be effectively removed [14,15].

Solar-heated oil absorbents have attracted interest as promising platforms for removal and recovery of viscous oils from water [16,17]. In general, solar irradiation in such materials lead to heating and concomitant lower oil viscosity, thereby enhancing absorption and recovery [18]. Reported photothermal oil absorbents include polypyrrole-polyethylene glycol-polymerized octadecylsiloxane-coated sponges [19], poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)/polypyrrole matrixes [20], carbon nanotube composites [21]. Most such materials, however, exhibit limited practical applicability due to complex synthesis procedures, limited scalability, and low performance.

Here, we present an innovative strategy for oil spill cleanup. We constructed via a simple synthesis scheme a composite porous matrix comprising polyurethane (PU) sponge treated with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and carbon dots (C-dots). PU is an inexpensive, commercially-available porous framework material, widely used in diverse industrial applications [22,23]. Due to its porosity, PU has been employed as an absorbent for different organic solvents and guest species [24,25]. Superhydrophobic PU sponges have been synthesized using different coating materials such as halloysite nanotube [23], PDMS [26], cement [27], nanoclay [28]; such materials have been mostly used for oil/water separation applications. C-dots constitute a class of carbonaceous nanoparticles, attracting significant scientific and technological interest due to their unique optical properties, simple synthesis routes, and environment-friendly properties [29–33]. The photothermal features of C-dots have been recently employed for solar-enabled water remediation [34]. In this work, we demonstrate that a composite C-dot-PDMS-PU sponge facilitates highly effective oil absorption. In particular, the embedded C-dots furnish sunlight-induced heat dissipation, resulting in lower oil viscosity and consequent greater oil uptake by the composite sponge. The C-dot-modified sponge can absorb large oil quantities in few minutes upon solar illumination, exhibiting high stability, and can be easily recycled allowing multiple uses.

p-Phenylenediamine (PPDA) was purchased from J&K Scientific, Israel. Polyurethane (PU) sponge was obtained from local supermarket, Israel. Castor oil was obtained from Alfa-Aesar. Ethyl acetate and ethanol were obtained from Bio-Lab Ltd., Israel. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) (Sylgard 184) and silicone oil were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of PPDA as reported previously [34]. Briefly, PPDA (0.4 g) was added to 40 mL ethanol and dissolved by stirring. The PPDA solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave followed by hydrothermal heating at 180 °C for 8 h. The C-dots solution was purified by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was further purified using silica column chromatography and C-dot powder was obtained through vacuum drying.

PU foam was cleaned before use by washing with ethanol and acetone successively followed by drying at 80 °C. 10 mg of PDMS prepolymer was dissolved in 10 mL of ethyl acetate/ethanol mixture (3:1 v/v). To this 10 mg of PDMS prepolymer solution, 15 mg C-dots were added and stirred for 1 h followed by addition of 1 mg curing agent. The PU sponges were dipped in the C-dot-PDMS solution for 4 h, and the resultant C-dot-PDMS-modified PU sponge was dried at 120 °C for 4 h. The dried sponge was washed with ethanol several times to remove unbound C-dots and PDMS. Samples of modified PU sponges were prepared by using 1.5 mg, 3 mg, and 6 mg of C-dots per mL of PDMS containing ethyl acetate/ethanol mixture.

Functional groups within C-dot-PDMS-PU were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Spectra were acquired through attenuated total reflectance (ATR)-FTIR using a Thermo Scientific, Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of sponge samples were obtained on a JEOL, SEM (Tokyo, Japan, JSM-7400F). For SEM analysis, the cross-sections of sponge samples were coated with chromium and imaged at different magnifications. Sponge hydrophobicity was determined by measuring water contact angle (WCA) using a contact angle meter (Attension Theta Lite, Biolin Scientific, Finland). The contact angles were measured by 5 μL water deposition on the surface of sponge at different locations, and average WCA was calculated. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a Q500 TA Instruments (USA). Thermal analysis was carried out by heating the sample from 30 to 600 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere. Thermal imaging of the sponges before and after light illumination was carried out by a thermographic camera (FLIR i7).

Densities of the composite PU sponges were determined by measuring weight and volume of five different samples at each modification. Density was measured by using the equation
(1)
D e n s i t y =
m
l w h
in which
m ,
l , w ,
a n d
h
correspond to weight, length, width, and height of sponge, respectively.

Sponge porosity was determined according to a previously published methodology [23,35]. Briefly, sponge samples were immersed in absolute ethanol and allowed to absorb for 24 h. Weights of the ethanol-soaked sponges were measured and porosity was calculated according to the equation
(2)
P o r o s i t y =
m
a
− m
ρ l w h
in which
m
and
m
a
correspond to the weight of sponge before and after ethanol absorption.
ρ
is the density of ethanol and
l ,
w ,
h
correspond to the length, width, and height of the sponge, respectively.

Experiments were carried out using castor oil (viscosity 723 mPa s at 25 °C) and silicone oil (viscosity 1000 mPa s at 25 °C). To investigate the oil absorption capacity, the sponges were placed in contact with oil/water mixtures and irradiation was applied using a solar simulator (Sciencetech, AX-LA125, ASTM Class-AAA) operating at a wavelength range of 350–1800 nm at 1 kW/m2 intensity. The oil absorption capacity (Q) of each sponge was determined by measuring the weight of sponge before and after absorption and calculated according to:
(3)
Q =
( W t − W i )
W i
where
Q
is oil absorption capacity per gram of sponge,
W i
a n d
W t
are initial weight, and weight after oil absorption, respectively. For the oil absorption experiment, the thickness of the sponge used was 3 mm. The absorbed oil was recovered by mechanical squeezing the sponge, and the weight of recovered oil was measured on laboratory balance. The oil absorption and recovery experiment was repeated for 50 cycles to evaluate the reusability of the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite.

The strategy for C-dot-mediated oil-spill cleaning is depicted in Fig. 1 . A commercially available polyurethane (PU) sponge was used as the host matrix for oil absorption. PU sponge is inexpensive, resilient, and provides substantial internal surface area available for guest molecules absorption [36,37]. As shown in the scheme in Fig. 1, the PU sponge was treated with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and C-dots prepared from p-phenylenediamine (PPDA); PDMS provided both a hydrophobic coating upon the PU surface that is essential for facilitating oil absorption, as well as serving as a binder for immobilization of the C-dots within the PU framework. As shown in Fig. 1 (inset), the C-dots constituted both chemical and physical cross linkers (a scheme outlining the detailed chemical network of the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite is depicted in Fig. S1). Previous studies have shown that hydroxyl functionalized C-dots were covalently cross-linked with PDMS [38], while PU foams displayed physical cross-linking with PDMS through hydrogen bonding [39]. Importantly, C-dots embedded within PU without co-addition of PDMS were found to leach out. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (middle), the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite was placed in contact with an oil/water mixture. Upon illumination with sunlight the embedded C-dots absorbed light energy, emitting heat. As a consequence, oil viscosity was reduced, resulting in efficient diffusion and uptake by the C-dot-PDMS-PU sponge. The composite sponge could be readily recycled after removal of the absorbed oil.

Structural and functional analyses of the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite are presented in Fig. 2 . The experiments were specifically designed to verify incorporation of the C-dots within the PDMS-PU host framework, and assess the effects of the C-dot-PDMS incorporation upon the surface properties of the sponge. The C-dots were prepared via conventional hydrothermal method employing PPDA as the carbon source, and their detailed characterization is presented in Fig. S2 [40,41]. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the PPDA C-dots, PDMS-PU sponge, and C-dot-PDMS-PU composite are depicted in Fig. 2A. The FTIR spectrum of the C-dots alone exhibit peaks in between 3150 cm−1 and 3350 cm−1 corresponding to amines and hydroxyl residues, C–H stretching vibrations around 2900 cm−1, and characteristic bands of carbon-bonded nitrogen and oxygen atoms between 1100 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 [41]. Notably, the FTIR spectrum of the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite in Fig. 2A retains the distinctive peaks of the C-dots (indicated by the arrows), confirming incorporation of a significant C-dot concentration within the PDMS-PU matrix.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images in Fig. 2B illuminate the effect of C-dot-PDMS incorporation upon the morphology of the PU sponge. The parent PU material comprises of a highly porous polymer network exhibiting smooth surfaces (left images). While the pore structure was retained in the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite (Fig. 2B, top right image), pronounced surface corrugation occurred upon treatment with C-dots-PDMS (Fig. 2B, bottom right image). The pore-surface modification underscores the significant effect of C-dot-PDMS incorporation within the PU host matrix; moreover, the extended internal rough surface in the C-dot-PDMS-PU, apparent in Fig. 2B, is important since it allows greater oil absorption by increasing oil-sponge contact and heat transfer from sponge to oil.

While Fig. 2 sheds light on the structural and microscopic properties of C-dot-PDMS-PU, we also examined the macroscopic, cooperative features of the composite sponge (Fig. 3 ). Water contact angle (WCA) results in Fig. 3A complements the SEM experiments (Fig. 2B), furnishing evidence for increased hydrophobicity of PU following modification with C-dot-PDMS. Fig. 3A shows that the WCA of the parent PU sponge – approximately 105° - significantly increased to 131° upon coating with PDMS, due to its pronounced hydrophobicity [42]. Fig. 3A further shows that in C-dot-PDMS-PU samples comprising different C-dot concentrations, the WCA slightly decreased, reaching 122° for C-dot-PDMS-PU prepared using 6 mg/mL C-dot solution (denoted C-dot 6-PDMS-PU). The inverse relationship between WCA and C-dot concentration is attributed to hydrophilicity of the C-dots. Overall, the WCA data in Fig. 3A attest to incorporation of a significant concentration of C-dots within the porous PDMS-PU matrix, consequently modifying surface hydrophobicity (while still retaining the hydrophobic nature of the C-dot-PDMS-PU surface, essential for oil absorption).

Fig. 3B presents thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of PU, PDMS-PU, and C-dot 6-PDMS-PU. The TGA curve of the parent PU sponge reveals two main degradation stages. The first degradation step from around 30 °C to 300 °C reveals approximately 30% weight loss and is ascribed to foam decomposition due to dissociation of urethane linkage to yield polyol and isocyanate [42,43]. A second degradation stage from 300 to 600 °C corresponding to depolycondensation of the PU framework [43,44]. The TGA curves of PDMS-PU (Fig. 3B, cyan curve) and C-dot 6-PDMS-PU (red curve) also feature a two-stage degradation process similar to pristine PU, indicating retention of the overall thermal profile of the PU sponges. However, PDMS-PU and particularly C-dot 6-PDMS-PU retain greater weight percentage at temperatures above 400 °C compared to the parent PU matrix (Fig. 3B). This result accounts for an enhanced thermal stability of the composites, and is likely related to PDMS-mediated immobilization of the C-dots within the PU sponge [34].

Fig. 3C outlines the density values recorded for the PU-based sponges, highlighting the effects of embedded PDMS and C-dots. The density of the parent PU host was 30 mg/cm3, gradually increasing when higher concentrations of C-dots were embedded reaching approximately 45 mg/cm3 for C-dot 6-PDMS-PU (Fig. 3C). Consistent with the WCA and TGA data in Fig. 3A and B, the C-dot-dependent increased sponge densities reflect the effect of C-dot encapsulation within the PU host matrix. A similarly significant cooperative effect, attributed to C-dot incorporation, is apparent in the sponge porosity analysis (Fig. 3D). The bar diagram in Fig. 3D shows that the porosity of the PU sponge was gradually reduced, from around 85% for the untreated PU sample to ∼75% in C-dot 6-PDMS-PU. The lower porosity of the composite sponges is consistent with the higher compactness recorded in the density measurements (Fig. 3C). It should be noted, however, that Fig. 3D indicates that pronounced porosity is still retained even in the composite sponge comprising the highest C-dot concentration (e.g. C-dot 6-PDMS-PU), an important feature for facilitating absorption of large oil quantities.

The functionality of the C-dot-PDMS-PU composite sponge as a solar-mediated oil removal platform is illustrated in Figs. 4 and 5
. The photothermal conversion properties of the C-dots stem from light absorbance in the visible region (Fig. S2). The pronounced absorbance has been ascribed to the presence of excess electrons on the C-dots’ surfaces which give rise to reduction of the HOMO–LUMO gap [45]. Immobilization of the C-dots within PU-PDMS matrix led to complete fluorescence quenching [34,46]; consequently, the energy absorbed by the C-dots upon irradiation likely dissipates only through nonradiative processes, giving rise to photothermal conversion and subsequent temperature increase [47].

Fig. 4 presents the temperatures of the C-dot-PDMS-PU upon illumination, with or without oil present, recorded using infrared (IR) camera [48]. In the experiments, C-dot-PDMS-PU samples were illuminated by a solar simulator (operating at an intensity of 1 kW/m2), and the sponge temperatures were determined at different times by using an IR thermograph camera (the corresponding thermal images are shown in the top row in Fig. 4). The temperature analysis of the sponges in air (i.e. no oil present, Fig. 4A) reveals dramatic increases in temperature of the C-dots-PDMS-PU composite as compared to pristine PU or PDMS-PU. For example, the maximal equilibrium temperature of the parent PU sponge [after 30-min irradiation] was 36°C, while the equilibrium temperature of the C-dot 6-PDMS-PU composite, reached within the same time duration, was almost 65°C (Fig. 4A). Notably, the graphs in Fig. 4A demonstrate that the temperature increase was directly related to the concentration of C-dots within the PDMS-PU matrix, confirming the central role of the C-dots in facilitating solar-induced heating by efficient light absorption.

Solar-irradiated temperature profiles of the sponges were also recorded in the presence of castor oil (Fig. 4B) and silicone oil (Fig. 4C). Fig. S3 confirms the sharp reduction of both castor oil and silicone oil viscosities upon temperature increase. Similar to the non-oil experiment in Fig. 4A, the inclusion of C-dots in the sponges contributed to significant heating effects, apparent in both oil-containing experiments. Interestingly, however, the graphs in Fig. 4B and 4C shows that the C-dot-PDMS-PU sponges attained maximal temperatures within 10 min of solar irradiation, subsequently decreasing upon further illumination. This observation is different from the temperature plateaus recorded in case of solar illumination for non-oil samples (i.e. Fig. 4A). The temperature reductions past the 10-min maxima are ascribed to heat uptake by the oils absorbed within the C-dot-PDMS-PU host matrix, resulting in lower overall sponge temperatures. Indeed, heat absorption by oil also likely accounts for the fact that the maximal temperatures recorded in case of C-dot-6-PDMS-PU with castor oil and silicone oil were at 58°C and 52°C respectively (Fig. 4B and C), lower than the corresponding maximal temperature of 65°C recorded in the non-oil samples (Fig. 4A).

Fig. 5 demonstrates practical application of solar-mediated oil extraction from water using the C-dot-PDMS-PU composites. In the experiment depicted in Fig. 5A, the C-dot-PDMS-PU sponge was placed upon an oil layer (mimicking an oil spill), and its effect on oil removal was assessed with and without solar irradiation. As dramatically apparent in Fig. 5A, oil was not extracted when the sponge was applied on the oil surface without irradiation (Fig. 5A, top row), while complete oil removal was accomplished following short irradiation (intensity of 1 kW/m2; Fig. 5A, bottom row).

Fig. 5B presents quantitative analysis of castor oil (Fig. 5B, i) and silicone oil (Fig. 5B, ii) absorption by C-dot-PDMS-PU, with and without solar irradiation. Castor oil and silicone oil have been widely employed as model systems in oil cleanup technologies [18,19,49]. In case of both oils, the highest absorption without solar irradiation occurred for PDMS-PU (Fig. 5B, green bars). This result accounts for the more hydrophobicity of the PDMS-coated PU framework [50,51], and the fact that co-addition of C-dots both reduced hydrophobicity of the internal PU sponge surface (i.e. Fig. 3A), as well as reduced sponge porosity (Fig. 3D). Dramatically different oil uptake was recorded upon solar irradiation (1 kW/m2). Upon irradiation of the sponges, the viscosity of the oil in contact with the matrix decreased, resulting in its significant diffusion into the sponge. Specifically, all C-dot-PDMS-PU sponges achieved significantly higher oil absorption upon irradiation compared to PDMS-PU, and this outcome was apparent for both oils examined (Fig. 5B, red bars).

Indeed, the differences between oil uptake with and without irradiation were significant (Fig. 5B, red bars vs. green bars). This important result accounts for the impact of C-dot encapsulation within the PDMS-PU sponges, as the C-dots absorb light, dissipate heat, and consequently affect lowering of oil viscosity and its uptake into the sponges. Interestingly, the bar diagrams in Fig. 5B indicate that the most pronounced oil uptake (for both castor oil and silicone oil) occurred in case of C-dot 3-PDMS-PU (i.e. sample prepared using 3 mg/mL C-dot solution), rather than C-dot 6-PDMS-PU. This result suggests that, despite the fact that the sponge temperature upon solar irradiation of C-dot 6-PDMS-PU appears higher than C-dot 3-PDMS-PU (i.e. Fig. 4), the lower density and higher porosity of C-dot 3-PDMS-PU compared to C-dot 6-PDMS-PU (Fig. 3C and D) likely play more significant roles in affecting oil uptake.

Interestingly, the oil uptake data in Fig. 5B further indicate that oil absorption capacity of C-dot 3-PDMS-PU for silicone oil (37 g/g) was slightly higher than castor oil (34 g/g). This result is seemingly unexpected, because the viscosity of silicone oil (1000 mPa s at 25 °C) is greater than castor oil (723 mPa s at 25 °C) [19]. The more efficient absorption of silicone oil might be due to Si–Si interactions between silicone oil and the PDMS layer [52]. Indeed, this interpretation is consistent with the more effective heat dissipation (leading to lower sponge temperature) recorded for the C-dot-PDMS-PU samples in the presence of silicone oil (Fig. 4C), likely reflecting the more pronounced interface between this oil and the PDMS-PU framework. Greater oil absorption capacity in case of C-dot 3-PDMS-PU compared to an unmodified PU sponge was also observed under natural sunlight (Fig. S4).

To evaluate the C-dot-PDMS-PU sponge as a potentially useful vehicle for oil cleanup, we measured the recycling capacity of the material (Fig. 5C). The bar diagram in Fig. 5C underscores excellent re-use capability of the composite upon many oil absorption/recovery cycles. In the recycling experiments, the oil absorbed was removed by manually squeezing the sponge. Notably, the C-dot 3-PDMS-PU sponge featured excellent oil recovery of ∼95% (i.e. approximately 95% of absorbed oil was recovered) in all cycles. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5C, re-use of the sponge was feasible even after 50 cycles of oil absorption and recovery, accounting for the mechanical and chemical resilience of the composite sponge and its recyclability. Indeed, even after 50 cycles, oil absorption decreased by just 10% from its initial capacity, further underscoring the excellent stability of the material. The utilized sponge sample was washed with hexane to remove residual oil prior to recycling. Spectroscopic analysis confirmed that C-dot leaching into the absorbed oils was negligible (Fig. S5). Selective uptake of an organic solvent mixed with water was also evaluated (Fig. 5D). In the experiment, a C-dot 3-PDMS-PU sponge was placed on the surface of hexane/water mixture, in which hexane was stained with Sudan III, and water dyed with methyl violet (photographs shown in Fig. 5D) [13]. After saturation, the sponge was squeezed to recover the absorbed hexane. Notably, no water was co-extracted by the C-dot 3-PDMS-PU sponge.

In conclusion, we developed a new composite matrix - C-dot-PDMS-PU - for oil-spill cleaning applications. C-dot-PDMS-PU integrates the porous framework of the PU sponge, hydrophobic PDMS coating which allows efficient absorption of oil, and C-dots for sunlight-induced heating. The new solar-mediated oil cleanup strategy exhibits important advantages in comparison with previously-reported approaches. The C-dot-PDMS-PU composites were prepared from readily-available, environmentally-benign, and inexpensive building blocks. The C-dots, in particular, which have not been previously employed in oil cleanup systems, are easily fabricated and are amenable for large scale production. The encapsulated C-dots are the core chemical constituent responsible for solar-mediated oil uptake, as these carbon nanoparticles convert absorbed solar radiation to heat, thereby decreasing oil viscosity and its consequent enhanced diffusion into the sponge. The excellent photothermal properties of the C-dots afford applicability of the of the C-dot-PDMS-PU system for removal of highly viscous oils – a particularly challenging task in most current technologies. Overall, the simple synthesis, good thermal and mechanical stability, and recycling capacity underscore the potential use of the new C-dot-PDMS-PU composite as an effective platform for oil spill cleanup.
Seema Singh: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Raz Jelinek: Data curation, Writing - original draft.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to Prof. Hanna Rapaport and Dr. Ifat Cohen for their assistance with contact angle measurements, and Dr. Arkadi Zilberman for help with the infrared thermal imaging. S.S. is grateful to the Marcus Fund of Ben-Gurion University of the Negev for a post-doctoral fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Chemical Structure of C-dot-PDMS-PU sponge; AFM image, UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra of C-dots; Oil viscosity vs temperature graph; graph showing oil absorption through sponge under natural sunlight; UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra of recovered castor oil and silicone oil.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.01.016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon materials as oil sorbents: a review on the synthesis and performance
J. Mater. Chem. A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Gupta, N.H. Tai, Carbon materials as oil sorbents: A review on the synthesis and performance, J. Mater. Chem. A. 4 (2016) 1550-1565. https://doi.org/10.1039/c5ta08321d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prescribed reading 3.2: long-term ecosystem response to the Exxon Valdez oil spill
Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.H. Peterson, S.D. Rice, J.W. Short, D. Esler, J.L. Bodkin, B.E. Ballachey, D.B. Irons, Prescribed reading 3.2: Long-term ecosystem response to the Exxon Valdez oil spill., Science. 302 (2003) 2082-6. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1084282.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-term environmental impact of oil spills
